title: Asthma — Acute Severe
oneLiner: Acute worsening of asthma with features of severe obstruction (PEFR 33-50% predicted) — can progress to life-threatening attack within minutes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax** — *single finding:* absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation + hypotension. Needle decompression before CXR.
- **Silent chest** — *single finding:* no wheeze at all (no air movement) → imminent respiratory arrest. Requires immediate senior review for intubation / IV bronchodilators.
- **Anaphylaxis** — *single finding:* stridor + urticaria + hypotension + history of trigger (food/drug). Treat with IM adrenaline before any asthma therapy.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** sudden-onset dyspnoea at rest, unable to complete sentences, cough, audible wheeze (or no wheeze if silent chest)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Respiratory rate >25 breaths/min
  - Tachycardia >110 bpm (if bradycardia → life-threatening)
  - Use of accessory muscles, intercostal/subcostal recession
  - Pulsus paradoxus (if measurable, >10 mmHg suggests severe)
  - Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) 33-50% of predicted or best
  - SpO₂ <92% (or 92-95% with supplementary O₂? — if on 15 L, still <92% → very severe)
  - Confusion, exhaustion, or cyanosis → pre-arrest
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **COPD exacerbation** — older age, chronic symptoms, smoking pack-years, worse expiratory phase
- **Pneumonia** — fever, purulent sputum, focal crackles/consolidation, raised WCC/CRP
- **Foreign body aspiration** — acute onset, focal monophonic wheeze, often in young children or elderly/dementia
- **LVF / cardiac asthma** — peripheral oedema, basal crackles, cardiac history, S3 gallop
- **Bronchiolitis** — <2 years, coryzal prodrome, expiratory wheeze with crackles, severe respiratory distress
- **Pulmonary embolism** — sudden pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs, raised D-dimer, low PaCO₂ (not high)
- **Splitter:** PEFR <33% + hypercapnia (PaCO₂ >4.6 kPa) — this is near arrest in asthma; in COPD same values less alarming. Also: eosinophilia >0.5×10⁹/L suggests asthma, not COPD.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - PEFR (if can perform) — best of 3
  - SpO₂ → target ≥94-98% (consider FiO₂)
  - ECG (tachy, RV strain, or arrhythmia)
  - Chest auscultation → silent chest is a red flag
- **Bloods:**
  - ABG (mandatory if SpO₂ <92% or life-threatening features) → look for PaO₂ <8 kPa, PaCO₂ normal or raised, pH low
  - FBC (eosinophilia), CRP, U&E
  - Theophylline level if on oral maintenance (therapeutic 5-15 mg/L)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (rule out pneumothorax, pneumonia, foreign body) — can be delayed if not immediately available, but do before escalation to NIV/ICU
- **Never-miss test:** **Arterial blood gas** — a normal or high PaCO₂ in a patient in distress is a preterminal sign (not "improving"). Do not skip it.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - High-flow oxygen (15 L via non-rebreather mask) to target SpO₂ 94-98%
  - Back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers (5 mg every 15-30 min) — up to 3 doses in first hour
  - Add ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg to first nebuliser only (unless very severe, then consider repeat)
  - Oral prednisolone 40-50 mg (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg if unable to take PO) — within first hour
  - Consider IV magnesium sulfate (1.2-2 g over 20 min) if life-threatening or poor response after 1h (adult dose)
  - Consider IV salbutamol or aminophylline if life-threatening features and no response — refer to senior / local policy
  - Call senior / ICU if any life-threatening feature, NEWS2 ≥5, or PaCO₂ >4.6 kPa despite therapy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue regular salbutamol nebulisers (e.g. 5 mg Q4H) with PRN breakthrough
  - Wean nebulisers to MDI + spacer when stable (patient can speak sentences, PEFR >50%)
  - Taper oral steroids: prednisolone 40-50 mg daily for 5-7 days (no taper needed if course ≤7 days)
  - Start/optimise inhaled preventer: beclometasone (or equivalent) via MDI + spacer
  - Monitor PEFR Q4-6H and chart trends
  - Chest physio if sputum retention
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Discharge on home inhalers (preventer + reliever) with written asthma action plan
  - Allergen avoidance if identified trigger
  - Smoke cessation counselling
  - Refer to asthma clinic / GP within 2 weeks
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline 1 L over 8h if NBM or vomiting (but maintain euvolemia — avoid fluid overload)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if expected admission >24h (unless bleeding risk)
  - Diet: normal unless severe (then NBM until stable for swallow)
  - Observations: hourly NEWS2 until stable, then 4-6 hourly after discharge from high dependency
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Salbutamol (nebulised) 5 mg Q15-30 min PRN in acute phase, then Q4H
  - Ipratropium bromide (nebulised) 0.5 mg as single dose in first hour (may repeat if very severe)
  - Prednisolone (oral) 40-50 mg once daily for 5-7 days
  - Hydrocortisone (IV) 200 mg stat then 200 mg Q6H if oral route unavailable (then switch to oral)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Magnesium sulfate (IV) 1.2-2 g over 20 min (adult)
  - IV salbutamol (250 mcg over 10 min then infusion 3-20 mcg/min) — [check local policy]
  - Aminophylline (IV) 5 mg/kg over 20 min (then infusion 0.5-0.7 mg/kg/hr) — only if no response, monitor levels [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Beta-blockers (even eye drops) can worsen asthma — avoid
  - NSAIDs may trigger bronchospasm in some asthmatics
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No major dose changes for short-term steroids; theophylline requires adjustment in liver disease/elderly
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Salbutamol overdose** — tachycardia, hypokalaemia, lactic acidosis. Do not exceed 5 mg nebulised more than 4 times per hour without senior review. **Aminophylline** — narrow therapeutic index; must check levels (5-15 mg/L) and monitor for arrhythmias.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory arrest
  - Pneumothorax / pneumomediastinum
  - Lactic acidosis from excessive beta-agonist
  - Hypokalaemia from beta-agonists
- **Chronic:**
  - Fixed airway obstruction / remodelling
  - ICU acquired weakness
  - Steroid side effects (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts) if repeated courses
sections.Prognosis: - Most patients with acute severe asthma improve within 24-48h with appropriate therapy. Mortality ~0.5% of attacks requiring admission. Poor prognostic markers: previous ICU admission, prior intubation, poor adherence to preventer.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always check a PEFR** on presentation and trend it — it's the best objective measure of severity.
2. **Give steroids within 1 hour of arrival** — every hour delay increases risk of mortality (NICE).
3. **If the patient is too breathless to speak, they are severe** — initiate the severe asthma protocol immediately, don't wait for ABG.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not confuse silent chest with “improving asthma”** — it's a sign of impending arrest.
2. **Do not use sedatives (e.g. benzodiazepines, opiates)** in acute asthma — they depress respiratory drive.
3. **Do not discharge patient until PEFR >70% predicted** and sustained for at least 24h on ward — early discharge increases readmission risk.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness despite rescue inhaler, new cyanosis/lips turning blue, confusion/drowsiness, or use of accessory muscles returns.
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48h of discharge, hospital asthma clinic within 2 weeks. Ensure they have a written asthma action plan and a spare preventer inhaler.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**
  - Any life-threatening feature (silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, exhaustion, confusion, PEFR <33%, SpO₂ <92%, PaO₂ <8 kPa, PaCO₂ normal/high)
  - Failure to improve after 1 hour of back-to-back bronchodilators
- **Call ICU / Crash team if:**
  - GCS <13, PaCO₂ >6.0 kPa, pH <7.25, hypotension unresponsive to fluids
  - Any need for non-invasive ventilation (rare in asthma) – this usually indicates pre-arrest
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate escalation
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L in absence of other cause
  - Respiratory rate >30 or <8

rawMarkdown:
## Asthma — Acute Severe
**One-liner:** Acute worsening of asthma with features of severe obstruction (PEFR 33-50% predicted) — can progress to life-threatening attack within minutes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax** — *single finding:* absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation + hypotension. Needle decompression before CXR.
- **Silent chest** — *single finding:* no wheeze at all (no air movement) → imminent respiratory arrest. Requires immediate senior review for intubation / IV bronchodilators.
- **Anaphylaxis** — *single finding:* stridor + urticaria + hypotension + history of trigger (food/drug). Treat with IM adrenaline before any asthma therapy.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** sudden-onset dyspnoea at rest, unable to complete sentences, cough, audible wheeze (or no wheeze if silent chest)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Respiratory rate >25 breaths/min
  - Tachycardia >110 bpm (if bradycardia → life-threatening)
  - Use of accessory muscles, intercostal/subcostal recession
  - Pulsus paradoxus (if measurable, >10 mmHg suggests severe)
  - Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) 33-50% of predicted or best
  - SpO₂ <92% (or 92-95% with supplementary O₂? — if on 15 L, still <92% → very severe)
  - Confusion, exhaustion, or cyanosis → pre-arrest

### Differential Diagnosis
- **COPD exacerbation** — older age, chronic symptoms, smoking pack-years, worse expiratory phase
- **Pneumonia** — fever, purulent sputum, focal crackles/consolidation, raised WCC/CRP
- **Foreign body aspiration** — acute onset, focal monophonic wheeze, often in young children or elderly/dementia
- **LVF / cardiac asthma** — peripheral oedema, basal crackles, cardiac history, S3 gallop
- **Bronchiolitis** — <2 years, coryzal prodrome, expiratory wheeze with crackles, severe respiratory distress
- **Pulmonary embolism** — sudden pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs, raised D-dimer, low PaCO₂ (not high)
- **Splitter:** PEFR <33% + hypercapnia (PaCO₂ >4.6 kPa) — this is near arrest in asthma; in COPD same values less alarming. Also: eosinophilia >0.5×10⁹/L suggests asthma, not COPD.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - PEFR (if can perform) — best of 3
  - SpO₂ → target ≥94-98% (consider FiO₂)
  - ECG (tachy, RV strain, or arrhythmia)
  - Chest auscultation → silent chest is a red flag
- **Bloods:**
  - ABG (mandatory if SpO₂ <92% or life-threatening features) → look for PaO₂ <8 kPa, PaCO₂ normal or raised, pH low
  - FBC (eosinophilia), CRP, U&E
  - Theophylline level if on oral maintenance (therapeutic 5-15 mg/L)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (rule out pneumothorax, pneumonia, foreign body) — can be delayed if not immediately available, but do before escalation to NIV/ICU
- **Never-miss test:** **Arterial blood gas** — a normal or high PaCO₂ in a patient in distress is a preterminal sign (not "improving"). Do not skip it.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - High-flow oxygen (15 L via non-rebreather mask) to target SpO₂ 94-98%
  - Back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers (5 mg every 15-30 min) — up to 3 doses in first hour
  - Add ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg to first nebuliser only (unless very severe, then consider repeat)
  - Oral prednisolone 40-50 mg (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg if unable to take PO) — within first hour
  - Consider IV magnesium sulfate (1.2-2 g over 20 min) if life-threatening or poor response after 1h (adult dose)
  - Consider IV salbutamol or aminophylline if life-threatening features and no response — refer to senior / local policy
  - Call senior / ICU if any life-threatening feature, NEWS2 ≥5, or PaCO₂ >4.6 kPa despite therapy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue regular salbutamol nebulisers (e.g. 5 mg Q4H) with PRN breakthrough
  - Wean nebulisers to MDI + spacer when stable (patient can speak sentences, PEFR >50%)
  - Taper oral steroids: prednisolone 40-50 mg daily for 5-7 days (no taper needed if course ≤7 days)
  - Start/optimise inhaled preventer: beclometasone (or equivalent) via MDI + spacer
  - Monitor PEFR Q4-6H and chart trends
  - Chest physio if sputum retention
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Discharge on home inhalers (preventer + reliever) with written asthma action plan
  - Allergen avoidance if identified trigger
  - Smoke cessation counselling
  - Refer to asthma clinic / GP within 2 weeks
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline 1 L over 8h if NBM or vomiting (but maintain euvolemia — avoid fluid overload)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if expected admission >24h (unless bleeding risk)
  - Diet: normal unless severe (then NBM until stable for swallow)
  - Observations: hourly NEWS2 until stable, then 4-6 hourly after discharge from high dependency

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Salbutamol (nebulised) 5 mg Q15-30 min PRN in acute phase, then Q4H
  - Ipratropium bromide (nebulised) 0.5 mg as single dose in first hour (may repeat if very severe)
  - Prednisolone (oral) 40-50 mg once daily for 5-7 days
  - Hydrocortisone (IV) 200 mg stat then 200 mg Q6H if oral route unavailable (then switch to oral)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Magnesium sulfate (IV) 1.2-2 g over 20 min (adult)
  - IV salbutamol (250 mcg over 10 min then infusion 3-20 mcg/min) — [check local policy]
  - Aminophylline (IV) 5 mg/kg over 20 min (then infusion 0.5-0.7 mg/kg/hr) — only if no response, monitor levels [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Beta-blockers (even eye drops) can worsen asthma — avoid
  - NSAIDs may trigger bronchospasm in some asthmatics
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No major dose changes for short-term steroids; theophylline requires adjustment in liver disease/elderly
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Salbutamol overdose** — tachycardia, hypokalaemia, lactic acidosis. Do not exceed 5 mg nebulised more than 4 times per hour without senior review. **Aminophylline** — narrow therapeutic index; must check levels (5-15 mg/L) and monitor for arrhythmias.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory arrest
  - Pneumothorax / pneumomediastinum
  - Lactic acidosis from excessive beta-agonist
  - Hypokalaemia from beta-agonists
- **Chronic:**
  - Fixed airway obstruction / remodelling
  - ICU acquired weakness
  - Steroid side effects (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts) if repeated courses

### Prognosis
- Most patients with acute severe asthma improve within 24-48h with appropriate therapy. Mortality ~0.5% of attacks requiring admission. Poor prognostic markers: previous ICU admission, prior intubation, poor adherence to preventer.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Always check a PEFR** on presentation and trend it — it's the best objective measure of severity.
2. **Give steroids within 1 hour of arrival** — every hour delay increases risk of mortality (NICE).
3. **If the patient is too breathless to speak, they are severe** — initiate the severe asthma protocol immediately, don't wait for ABG.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not confuse silent chest with “improving asthma”** — it's a sign of impending arrest.
2. **Do not use sedatives (e.g. benzodiazepines, opiates)** in acute asthma — they depress respiratory drive.
3. **Do not discharge patient until PEFR >70% predicted** and sustained for at least 24h on ward — early discharge increases readmission risk.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness despite rescue inhaler, new cyanosis/lips turning blue, confusion/drowsiness, or use of accessory muscles returns.
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48h of discharge, hospital asthma clinic within 2 weeks. Ensure they have a written asthma action plan and a spare preventer inhaler.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**
  - Any life-threatening feature (silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, exhaustion, confusion, PEFR <33%, SpO₂ <92%, PaO₂ <8 kPa, PaCO₂ normal/high)
  - Failure to improve after 1 hour of back-to-back bronchodilators
- **Call ICU / Crash team if:**
  - GCS <13, PaCO₂ >6.0 kPa, pH <7.25, hypotension unresponsive to fluids
  - Any need for non-invasive ventilation (rare in asthma) – this usually indicates pre-arrest
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate escalation
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L in absence of other cause
  - Respiratory rate >30 or <8
